pages.text:
Most of the museum was rather heavy-going because our French wasn't good enough. I preferred the Stonehenge museum. If it rains less tomorrow we'll go on a guided tour.
  We found __campsite of the white sand__ — a lovely beach. Crowded. It poured & poured rain. After supper there was an amazing rainbow as the sun came out & the sky cleared. We went for a lovely walk along the beach — a beautiful pink lilac sky. Then we read.
__Tuesday__
Sand everywhere, including in bed. Euch. Feet sticky. I had a shower & bravely washed my hair. Sunshine. It dried quickly.
  At 11.30 we started the English tour of the most westerly of the Carnac sites & the heavens kept opening. Cold & wet. Cold & wet. We had a good guide.
  We looked at the enclosure afterwards [illegible]

where farm houses had been built in among the stones, using the stones. There are nearly 3,000 stones left. There used to be double that number. It is reckoned that every house around has a stone in from Carnac. They stood up right [—] local stones [and] an enclosure at the end for worship in each of the 3 sites. Celtic words used. Celts arrived 600 BC (Stones 6,000 BC).
Men = stone.
Men-hir: high Stone
Dol-men: stone table.
Menhirs are sites of the living.
Afterwards back in van, set off east. Sunny lunch in lay by. Pick blackberries.

captions: ALIGNEMENTS DE CARNAC
pages.text:
On + we picked black-berries at lunch time. Simon sat in the sun.
On arrival at [?Flatter] __Malestroit__ municipal campsite * * * * *
we wandered into town on our bikes, chained them infront of the church, looked aroun[d] the town. + shopped.

[Glued-in photo: standing stones at Carnac at sunset, captioned "ALIGNEMENTS DE CARNAC"]
[Glued-in ticket: CARNAC, Alignements de / VCI TR CARNAC VISITE CONF INDIV / VCI Adulte - Anglais / le 26/08 a 11:30 / Menec ouest / Valable le 26/08/2014 / 5.00 EUR / Vendu le 26/08/2014 a 11:03 NI ECHANGE NI REMBOURSE / C213/PATI 240690052393]

Simon bought a purse, Eve bought gloves. We went back to the van (over the bridge across the river) + returned to town to have supper at the Grain of Salt. At 8.30. we went to a fabulous organ concert in the Church by Prof Mickael Gaborieau. Really amazing. There was a video link so we could see his hands, feet + the music clearly.

__Wednesday__ It's not raining but the weather didn't look hopeful. We had originally thought of spending 2 nights here, but with rain forecast for the afternoon, we moved out of the camp + set off on a bike ride. The campowner's wife said it wouldn't rain. We cycled N.W along the Brest-Nantes Canal. Then William rang to say his passport had been in the stolen Knapsack + he needed a new one for Majorca next week + wanted our passport details. We reached St. Andrew of the Rock (lovely- well-cared for church) + had coffee + cycled home, a bit of a gentle uphill, along the voie-verte - disused railway line. Lunch back in Malestroit. We didn't expect much when the mothy bar man said only sandwich or kebab, but the kebab turned out to be a big hamburger sandwich w/ chicken stew + salad - rather good.

back at the van we texted William with the details, had coffee & left. Onto __[?Bréon] en [?Ail]__, we walked around before bed. There seemed to be either gypsies or poor people on the campsite. One woman had a washing machine + tumble drier on a huge wooden pallette outside. Rain, rain. Mud but no litter but all their rubbish was in bags by the washrooms. Most odd.

__Thursday__
More rain, gradually clearing during the day. We drove thro or past [?Mencon], [?Cartrouges], the round Argenten to the Chambois + looked at the town + newly laid wreaths. 20 years ago we met an American on a beach in Devon (1944 for Juno? beach) now we see French memorials after 70 years, August 2014. On Aug 19-22 the Amer, Canadians, French, Brits & Poles closed the Falaise rocket. The gap was 30k, then down

captions: Montormel Memorial
pages.text:
to 3 k while attacked by Poles on Hill 262 (ht) the Polish Hills, the Germans fled along the Corridor of Death. Horses (only 30% of the German army was modernised) +

[glued-in postcard/leaflet — printed text:]
__MONTORMEL__
It's raining in Montormel
And yet, it's summer ;
Heaven is weeping over events former.
It's raining in Montormel...

Il pleut sur Montormel
Et pourtant c'est l'été
Le ciel pleure le passé
Il pleut sur Montormel...

[handwritten caption:] Montormel Memorial

men rotted & were bull-dozed into pits by the Brits: 100,000 Germans, 50,000 escaped, 40,000 prisoners, 10,000 killed. Flies & smell were terrible. Land didn't recover until 1954.

    We shopped at le Clerke & went on [?**RS] to __bigne le Foret__ to camp at __St. Paul__, It's a very pretty valley. You could walk up into the town. A "trout stream" (8fat) runs thro the camp Good showers. About 2½ hours from the Channel Tunnel. It rained in the night. A beautiful old town. I'd like to walk here.

    __Friday__
    We left at 8.35. For Charleval + petrol [?Salon] Fortunately the machine took a card. We reached the train on time & had lunch in the train. On to Whittlesey St., where I knocked very loudly on the door. We brought pudding with us.

    __Saturday 30th August.__
    After a night & lunch with the William Kirby we set off for [?Tinds] on the way home.

__Saturday Oct 3[rd] 4th__
    Thomas is in India. Naomi's about to have a baby. At 11.30 we set off with Austin to __Wolverley__ to camp for the night & go on a bike ride.
    We found a pitch where we can see the Playground – __Wolverley__ **** v.clean loos. In the afternoon we walked around the village & saw the outside of the church Austin played with a girl called Ruby who was also here with her grandparents Lovely sunshine after a rainy morning. The night was freezing cold. Austin disappeared in his sleeping bag.

    __Sunday Oct 5th.__
    We set off at 9.15 on the bike ride & got back at 2.45. We cycled to Kidder & had coffee. Then on to Stourport. V.attractive Tontine Hotel & appartments for sale. Georgian, from start of canal hotels. Built by Brindley, it was a hamlet calls

called Mitton, renamed by Brindley. But nowadays v. cheap fairground, incomprehensible language "... Bab!" v. poor [?pess] people. We bought lunch for £4.95 - a roast. Inedible chicken, teeth bounced as if chewing car tyre. Spat it out & have ready in doggy bag for Trader's supper. As bad as a meal near Garsford or the English lunch on the French canal.
	Austin (6) was BRILLIANT on the 15m like ride.
	The campsite used to be an American WW2 army hospital. Out of 10,000 patients only 4 died.
	Back to the camp for [?tea/teas] then set off home.

Oct 11th 2014 Saturday
	8.20. Simon, Eve & Tinder left for

Mandarin - stone, slate, granite for lights at Trumpington. Gorgeous stones for bathrooms & kitchens. Eve telephoned Kate & Bryony to tell them about it because they are putting in bathrooms & kitchens [illegible]

at the farm. There is a showroom at Monmouth.
	Then on to Annette who took us out to lunch at Auberge. Big John's son is getting married. We had chicken/sausage (Eve) & Guinea fowl (Annette & Simon). Delicious. And crème brulée, raspberry sorbet & chocolate mousse.
	Then we went to tea with John & Pamela. Money shortage.
	On to __Kenninghall__ * * * * * South Lodge.
	Tiny. Shed for washroom. Good.
	Thunder, lightening, rain. The owner told us there'd be a concert of local music talent at the church at 7.30. We didn't really want to go out. But we did & it was super - David Knier played guitar & sang, John Loker played the mouth organ. They were terrific.
	The pub landlord also played the mouth organ. There is an arrangement that the

pub can go to the church to pray & the church can go to the pub – the Red lion to pee. The food is supposed to be very good.
    A freezing (almost) cold night. It's really too cold to camp now. Trouble with the room heater.

[glued-in ticket: RoofAid presents Raise the Roof! St Mary's Church, Kenninghall Saturday 11th October 2014 Doors open 7:00pm for 7:30pm Admit One: £5 in advance, £6 on the door Ticket No: 142]

Sunday
    Visited St. Mary's South Lopham. 5 tiered tower, Norman. 900 year old chest (dug out). The elephant & castle on the now end is from the Beaufort family. 6 Betts on the

war memorial & 2 families with 5 names. Now sun breaking through the fog, so we might walk in Thetford Forest.
    There's now a bridge near the church to replace what used to be a ford between 2 springs, one of R. Ouse, one of R. Waveney. When there's a flood, Norfolk becomes an island.
    We drove on to woods & walked next to the wood on a track with fields on one side, growing onions & turnips? Motorcycles passed. It was a super walk with Tinder. Then back into the van & back to B'ham for tea.

April 10th 2015 Friday.
        Simon, Eve + Tinder left at 6.30pm. at
Thomas + Naomi at Lyttleton Rd, William +Bryony
just moved to Bromyard. Peter + Nana about
to move Calcutta → us → London. John in
Swansea about to move to Cardiff. Charles +
Jenny have just bought a house in Oxford.
I have been spending alot of time in Suffolk
with Annette who is dying.
We drove to __Tonge__ [?H#*] near Nottingham, on
the Cloud Cycleway. Thou the gate wewere
on the cycleway. Warm day.
Saturday   cold morning.  We cycled 3 miles up
to the Trent River, disused, often embanked
railway line thru fields.
        We drove to Nottingham + had lunch
in a pub next to the Kanal. Good, Selaft!
Stag party? next to us. Not many greghans
in restaurant.
        We walked up thru Gatehouse to
the Castle museum. It started as a Norma[n]

Castle. Charles I raised his banner there 67
at Start of Civil War. Cromwell had it razed.
Rebuilt, burnt, rebuilt (Reform Act).
Lovely alabaster + costume museum.
Tea back at the van + we have driven to
another field – Pleasleyfarm campsite * *.
We went along a Roman road out of
Nottingham – slow + bordered by buildings.
Nottingham used to be called Snottingham.
The Snots.
The farm is a sort of depot for storing
large lorries. Windswept.
                Sunday
                Cold feet all
                night.
                After breakfast
                on to
                Hardwick
                Hall.

"More class than well in __Hardwick Hall__. We looked at the gardens + the mixed old Hall. Tints kept pulling on the [?lead]. Very cold wind. Ferrari's + Poshes on the carpet. Charles [?Batto]. Peter called. Boss of Hardwick's granddaughter Arbella kept a prisoner because a threat to [?Eliza]. Bloody furnishings. Well laid out + cared for.

Off 2:30 pm after a good lunch We looked round house + now going home.

captions: THE RIVER WYE SYMONDS YAT
pages.text:
Friday April 24th
   We left at 5.30 + reached The White House at Ross at 6.45. It's on the side of the hill overlooking the Wye. In the 1700s a benefactor called Kyrle did alot of good works beautifying the town. The B+B owner spent ages mentioning every restaurant in Ross + whether they [?are good] [illegible]

We walked from this view-point Yat Rock down to river + up again.

hrs.

They wd. take dogs. Tinder was allowed in the bedroom & given a sausage for breakfast. (John is embarrassed that we have called the dog Tinder because it is a sex website. People laugh when they hear us calling the dog.) Supper in van

__Saturday__
Simon & I spent ages in the second hand book shop. John arrived at 11.15  We had coffee in the van wandered in town & lunch in the van. Then we went in John's whizzy turquoise car to Symonds Yat. Fantastic views. Strenuous walk. Supper at B+B owner (Judy's) brother's Bistro in town. Good Portugese cook. Tagine. B+B v. comfortable but carpets grubby. John drove to Symonds Yat, Simon drove back. V. powerful, noisy engine.

__Sunday April 26__[th]
Another huge breakfast & on to Tintern Abbey. Gorgeous ride thro spring green colours. V. cold. Peaceful Abbey, well worth a visit. After coffee John set off back to the Mumbles & Simon & Eve & Tinder had lunch in the van at Westbury Court Garden.

captions: TINTERN
Cistercian Monks, 11[th]B. Liked isolated places. R. Wye
pages.text:
Cistercian Monks, 11[th]B. Liked isolated places. R. Wye

We drove over hills from Wye to Severn.

captions: Enjoy Westbury Court Garden — Rediscover a formal Dutch water garden (National Trust brochure cover, glued in)
pages.text:
Westbury Court Garden originally laid out 1696-17[illegible]
Restored with original plante 1967 N.T.
(Before Capability Brown)    lovely tulips &
On home for tea.              apple trees

Friday 29th May.
Simon, Eve, Tinder left at 6 for Coughton
Court. We are in a farmer's field. ****
Good foot path walks.
__Saturday__
We walked about 3 miles by the river
& left Tinder in the van. Lunch in the
courtyard block, then explored the house
& garden [?for] of the Throckmorton family.
lived here for 600 years. Catholic. One
woman had 19 children who all survived
Owen priest's hole. Lovely house & garden.
Of the 13 people implicated in the gun-
powder plot, 7 were related to the
Throckmortons.
We suppered in the van. It was cold.
__Sunday__ We went to see a nearby NT [illegible]
dovecote. [?If] with ladder intact, it was
amazing. The bird perches were staggered
in the way they pointed.
We walked by the river &
got home for lunch.

captions: Rushton Triangle Lodge 1593. Built by [?Tresham] RC. after being freed from prison.
pages.text:
Saturday June 20th.
4 of the 5 boys are moving house this summer. This week John is holidaying in L.A. William has just moved into the farm. Charles is storing stuff in our garage before moving into a room while doing up his new house. Peter moves to London next week. John moves [from]

[Aerial photograph of a triangular building with surrounding grounds]

[Rotated caption beside photo:] Rushton Triangle Lodge 1593. Built by [?Tresham] RC. after being freed from prison.

Swansea to Cardiff.
        At. 10.30. Simon, Eve Charles & Tinder left for Naseby. It's a good viewpoint.
Prince [?Charles] [strikethrough: Rupert] cavalry chased the New Army cavalry away & the Cromwell [?'s] infantry killed the Cavalier infantry.
        Then on to the Triangular Lodge.
A most odd, small building built by RC. He was the grandfather of a gunpowder plotter, married to a Throckmorton.
        I had had a sore throat for 2 nights before the trip + suddenly at Naseby it became a cold. When we got to Top End*** campsite at Little Staughton, I stayed inside while Simon + Charles sat outside + walked. Half the site is given over to a Ford Corsair rally. Very serious business. Simon + Charles are spending much time discussing Morgan engineering.
        We had a quiet night.

__Sunday__ We went for a walk to see the outside of a [?Pt] Priory but I had to turn back because I was tired. The others returned soon because the gate was locked.

    After lunch Simon is driving home in the van + Charles is taking Tinder + me on to Ansted where we will meet up with Kate + Lizzie, for Annette's cremation + funeral.

__Saturday July 25th__
__Just after 10 Simon & Eve took Tinder to kennels (£310 for 3 weeks) & set off to [?x x x x] Kent. V. Heavy traffic. Got to__ __Stalisfield__ __again. Great improvement in pub food under new owners. Just got a table at 6.30 pm. Need to book a meal ahead next year. As soon as we arrived I put the kettle on (5.10.) + then did yoga for 10 min + felt much less stiff.__

  __Sunday__ We set off at 8.45. & reached the Channel Tunnel car park at 9.10. An hour

early, but yesterday queues had been 5 hrs. 1/20 closed, Operation Stack to cope with waiting lorries. French trouble has been caused by both rioting asylum seekers + French tyre-burning [?for] ferry strikes.

   On towards Laon. The rain got [?x x] heavier & heavier. We reached Village [?Waten] at Suzy, a campsite with an American Rodeo/Wildwest Theme. Camping in wagons & tipis. Nice. We had a meal which had a good first course. Not good food really. Lots of girls who'd like horses.

Monday in the morning croissant for Simon + a baguette. No rain but overcast.
On to Laon, with the cows looking out. We parked + walked up lots of steps — perfect sized steps — the best I've ever walked up. Laon Cathedral is beautiful, especially the Tower of the crossing from inside! Built in 55 years. All of a piece. Laon was the Carolingian capital, on a hill, easy to defend. Walking along the streets is nice. Coffee

x x x  Etangs du Moulin

captions: Laon Cathedra built 1220 - 1264. Corners of towers cut away, so platforms for cows to look out.
pages.text: Laon Cathedra built 1220 - 1264. Corners of towers cut away, so platforms for cows to look out.

captions: Gorgeous chocolate
pages.text:
We drove thro the Laonnaise hills, skirting Champagne, then crossed a series of rivers such as Aisne, Marne + Seine on Droats. Stopped at a le Clerks. Reached Trainel Municipal Park -- rundown but clean.

Gorgeous chocolate

captions: Vezelay on Yonne
pages.text: Tuesday  We had a lie in + set off to Vezelay by quite complicated main roads. At the carpark, halfway down the hill,

I had to wait while Simon went off to find the €6. We had lots of time to look at the west wall, 2 of them, inner + outer, + all the capitals. At one time 1000 pilgrims were burned when the cathedral caught fire. It was involved with crusades. Richard the [lion]heart visited.

    We started with lunch + I had chocolate waffles gou[?]ffles, for pudding.
*****
    On to Chaumot, near Corbigny run by a very relaxed Dutchman. He bought a station house near here 25 years ago + this campsite 14 years ago. The loos are brilliant – easy to clean – loo, bin, brush off the floor. It's on Canal Nivernais, so we can bike ride tomorrow. It started to rain when I was in the loo + has gone on + on. Sodden cyclists keep arriving...
Wednesday July 29th.

We are on a sort of island with a bridge at one end. There was a railway here once which interchanged with the canal. The next village has "mines" in

the name - silver mine. More rain in the night. The washing machine is in the men's wash area. A nice man helped me by plugging it in. Only 35 minutes - just what u need for a camp site. When we arrived the levelling block kept bouncing back as Simon tried to ride up it + the kind Dutchman came out with a peg (ours were not to hand because no children ∴ no tent.)

	Campsite owners save themselves a lot of trouble by not having pitches. The rule here is "one night cycle tents" in the middle - everyone else around the outside." I do like Dutch-owned camp sites.
I hung the washing in the van. It rained most of the morning + stopped about noon. After lunch we set off northwards past Dirol to [?Monceau]. For part of the journey we were taken off the tow path + onto back lanes. A lovely ride about 12m. At [?Monceau] we bought pate + a tart.
	There are cows in the field next to us. Nice Dutch neighbours. Our neighbour is a family friend of the owner. He comes at the beginning of summer to help set up the camp (not plumbing) electrics, lawnmow[er]

+ he + his wife can stay on for free. He's on site to mend things, including children's toys - go-kart. His wife has a zimmer.
	They tell us about a concert about to happen on the 3rd night after our arrival at which people can join in. He sings a Dutch song.
	__Thursday__ Day of concert. We take lunch with us on a bike ride. My knees hurt every pedal by the end of the ride. V. painful getting in + out of van. 12 miles, past potter we visited years before up light - staircase - of 16 locks. We reach the top + Simon goes 2k on the other side to see our old campsite. There are a boy + girl at abt of the locks, manning the gates. Boaters are not given their own paddles. Nivernais built float logs for Paris firewood, 1784 - 1835.
Back at the camp we go to the concert 9 - midnight. V. cold. Nearly everyone else went off to bed before the end. We wore socks, shawls + blankets. Wonderful boogie playing by a 17 yr old. + another brilliant pianist + super singer, older + fatter than I am but a marvellous voice. And a good drummer + blond singer. Beatles, Dutch, French songs. Imagine, Bob Dylan.
Corrine, singer, part owner of camp w/ Reynard, Dutchman

captions: Paray-le-Monial (Saône-et-Loire) Burgundy Christ in Glory, North Portal, [?Crche]
pages.text:
Friday we set off about 9.45 or 10, after filling w/ water + emptying loo.

Paray-le-Monial

[three photos of church/abbey interior]

Paray-le-Monial (Saône-et-Loire) Burgundy
Christ in Glory, North Portal, [?Crche]

off to Intermarché, [?Mouskatère], better than he Clerke earlier in the week. Rabbit pâté good. We had lunch on a Canal, le Canal lateral de la Loire, next to an Abbey. On to Paray-le-Monial. This has many women's religious orders represented. Simon didn't come into one of the most interesting churches with this theme because it had a "no shorts" sign. The main Abbeychurch has lovely [?Cotswold] stone. No nave, designed by Hugo from Cluny who was influenced by Mozarabic in Spain. A well-kept old town. We cycled back to the campsite. A boys' camp packed up. Lots more Dutch. Cows. I looked in a cabin while it was being cleaned. The campsite felt airless. During the night it rained + rained.
Saturday August 1st.
It still rained in the morning. 10.30. We set off + had elevenses at the Abbey of Dieu Donné Abbaye [?Bènisitions] Dieu, gorgeous steeple + roof of Beaune, coloured tiles. Benedictine. 1135

captions: Saint Jean-Saint Maurice sur Loire
pages.text:
laures Fracasso's father  03-85-84-05-22
Laure was our au pair about 22 years ago. Her family (1 real sister + 7 adopted siblings) her parents came to visit for a w/end then. Both Laure + her father have hare-lips.
   When we get near where they used to live, I got Simon to stop the van. I went into the nearest shop - a sit-on lawnmower shop. I said "Do you know the Fracasso family?" + the man pointed to his lip, which was also a hare lip, & said "Like this?" + I said "yes".

So we now have [?the] Fracasso's [tel.] no. It was 77 amazing.
   We reached St. Maurice + looked all around the village - a tower + a church w/ frescoes. V. steep streets. Views of the damned lake. Then we motored on ... + on ... + on. Nearly or 3 hours, trying to find campsite. We have now reached a v. entertaining one, bursting with noisy children. On a lake, fishing, swimming, badminton, bikes, skate boards. All young, whereas Chaumont was mostly old. [We] are parked opposite the Loos, camouflaged with bamboos + we see parents + children coming + going. There's a deli + soapseller. We buy chips from the bar. A good end to the day. __Arpheuilles__ at __St. Paul de Vézelin__. (v. good for families)
__Sunday__ There were 2 layers of mist on the reservoir. It's lovely + [warm] today - the first day we've had breakfast outside. We drove off from the Gorges of the Loire south. Bigger hills, [Saw] past St Etienne. Simon bought lunch in St. Maurice de Lignon + stopped soon after in a village ringed round a huge

pages.sketch_desc: Printed map (part of glued-in leaflet) showing the Greenway route alongside La Loire river; labels include La Loire, Valence, Brives-Charensac (Old station, Campsite Brives), and Latour. Route runs north–south with a green line indicating the cycleway.
pages.text:
green. Men are dismantling a village marquee after yesterday's festival.  __La Besse__

On to __Puy en Velay__, campsite 3 k away at Brives Charensac. Yerghot.
__Monday__ At 10 we left on the bike ride. Brives → Solignac. Arrived at 1 + had picnic lunch. Every pedal + push, steady incline. Only 1½ hours to get back, free-wheeling nearly all the way. The cycleway surface is formed from chippings laid on the original railway ballast and generally easy. The tunnels are well lit with occasional places where water falls from the roof. We saw no food at Solignac.

[printed leaflet glued in]
__HISTORY:__
The railway line from Le Puy-en-Velay to Langogne came into service in 1912 to provide a link between the industrial centres of the St Etienne region and those of the Cévennes.
However, since 1981, trains have deserted this route and the rails have been removed to create a pleasant path which attracts walkers, cyclists and mountain bikers.

__DESCRIPTION:__
The starting point of the Greenway (North–South direction) is situated 4 km from Le Puy-en-Velay on the way out of Brives Charensac station (altitude 600 m). A gentle climb (2%), easy for all, takes you to Solignac-sur-Loire (altitude 850 m). The path goes through 5 tunnels, all lit up, and a metal-spanned viaduct, 184 m long.

pages.sketch_desc: Continuation of printed map from left page showing the full Greenway route. Runs from Solignac/Loire (alt. 857 m) at top through tunnels (Veneyres, Pradeaux, Malpas, Riou, Taulhac) and stations (Coubon-Yolhac) south to Le Puy-en-Velay. Arrows and km markers indicate direction and distances. Practical information legend on right side.
pages.text:
[handwritten text top-left, partially obscured by map]
[?We] [?restaurant] + [?washed] [?the] [?by] [?hands]. We ate at the camp restaurant. Good lasagna. Sorbet, [?and] vanilla ice cream + [?had] caramel ice cream + chocolate sauce

[printed leaflet/map continuation]
[Map labels:] Solignac/Loire, Old station Solignac/Loire alt. 857 m, Km 13.8, Aubenas, Mende, Gagne viaduct, Tunnel de Veneyres, __Veneyres__ Km 12.1, Tunnel de Pradeaux, Malpas alt. 858 m, Tunnel de Malpas, [?Coubon], Cussac-sur-Loire alt. 651 m, Old station __Coubon-Yolhac__ alt. 720 m, Km 4.9, Latour Km 3.8, Tunnel de Riou, Le Riou Km 8.6, Tunnel de Taulhac, Le Puy-en-Velay alt. 621 m, St-Etienne, Lyon

[Practical information box:]
Greenway —
Main access point
Secondary access point
Nearby town or village
Bicycle hire
No access for motorized vehicles
Emergency telephone number +
Eat before feeling hungry and drink before feling thirsty
Remember that the tunnels may be cold
Respect the Greenway, the environment and surrounding property
Keep dogs on leads

captions: Aiguille rock seen from the banks of the Borne
pages.text:
Aiguille rock seen from the banks of the Borne

I [?rea] remember seeing the allotments from the rock on our last visit.

__Monday~Tuesday__
 It was so hot that it should have rained heavily in the night, with thunderstorms, but it didn't. There was a bit of rain in the morning. We cycled along the Voie Verte for 5 km to [?Auys] thro suburbs & past the view above. We climbed up to see the cathedral. You walk up the hill, then there are 60 steps until you reach the front of the building, then up another 75 steps to level with the top of the arch * – the walker comes out almost under the crossing at the front of the nave – very unusual.
	 I bought lace & we walked around the town steep cobbles & had a good lunch. The buildings are 5 stories high. It [?is] a rich town.

captions: Below the Holy Cross (15th century) lies the 'apparition' stone which is said to be part of the sick sanctuary. According to legend, in a dream, a sick woman received the order to climb the mount. When she reached the top, she noticed a flat stone where she lay down and fell asleep. Around her gathered "a multitude of holy and saints, and above them, a woman of royal dignity and saints, and above her clothes, everything about her was majesty; her face, the woman of royal dignity about her was extraordinary beauty". When she awoke, she was healed. Today,
pages.text:
[Two photographs glued in. Top photo shows a town square with a large Romanesque cathedral facade, crowds of people, and tall stone buildings — likely Le Puy-en-Velay. Bottom photo shows a recumbent stone statue of a figure, with a printed caption card attached:]

Below the Holy Cross (15th century) lies the 'apparition' stone which is said to be part of the sick sanctuary. According to legend, in a dream, a sick woman received the order to climb the mount. When she reached the top, she noticed a flat stone where she lay down and fell asleep. Around her gathered "a multitude of holy and saints, and above them, a woman of royal dignity and saints, and above her clothes, everything about her was majesty; her face, the woman of royal dignity about her was extraordinary beauty". When she awoke, she was healed. Today,

This is another good campsite for families. Swimming pool & cycling. At 9.30, pm there are 26 children in the playground & field.

~~Tuesday~~ Wednesday
We motored south west & I got very very very hot in the passengers seat. We had sausages in Grandvieu, a rather sad town where most of the shops were closed - either the owners were on holiday or they were being sold, [?+]. Most odd mannkin/puppets all over. __Valde[?]__ [?Saures]. Spooky. We reached __Entrayques__. The [?campsite] computer knew that we'd been here 2006 + 2010. The [?last] 2006 visit was when Simon & Charles spent hours playing with a plastic bottle & stick in the rapids. I really like Ent... the flat level bridge takes you [?to] right into town. Late, about 8.30, we walked up into town & saw trestle tables & lots of people eating in the square & local produce on sale.

__Thursday__
A lovely day, reading all day, Simon a book on Turing & I a Clancy whodunnit. I did a 9 kilo wash, everything, that was dry by lunch time. A father & son cycling duo wearing orange & black are in the [?pitch] [?happened]

pages.sketch_desc: A simple hand-drawn sketch of a bridge, showing the upstream elevation with pointed arch buttresses and the downstream side with square towers (or bases). Labeled 'upstream' above the sketch with an arrow. The number '13' appears in a circle beside the sketch, appearing to be an annotation reference.
pages.text:
I don't know how they do it. I'm too hot lying in the shade.

Before supper we walked on the island that gets flooded, & admired all the trees. After we went to bed there was super disco music for dancing until very late.... but Simon was snoring.

Friday We have been getting up later & later which is nice. After breakfast we walked up to the market in town & bought food, bought a girl's dress to copy for the 5 granddaughters. We walked to the 'old Bridge' [sketch of bridge with label: upstream] (13). It is a magnificent building with pointed buttresses on the upstream side & square towers downstream, or the bases for [what were] square towers. It has withstood snow & flood for 700 years.
The oldest street in town is hideously decrepit with rotting buildings for sale, which may well be flooded at times.
At tea time we invited an Irishman from the

captions: Abbatiale Sainte-Foy, tympan du Jugement dernier (début du XIIe siècle)
pages.text:
opposite pitch. He & his son are cycling St. Clau[?]hyons. He's off to Nepal in the [crossed out] winter.

We chose a restaurant on the square which advertised puddings made in the restaurant. It was a very good choice. Samu[el] had tripe + [Alison?]. I had salmon pasta. Samin[?] had chef's pastry for [just?] – as adored by me + I had choc. [Sou?] [Fondant?]. Both delicious.

Thunderstorms in the night. V.v. hot. The pharmaciene at the next table (with 2 her sons) was V. nice + invited us to look her up at the pharmacy on [bar?] our next visit. She knows local restaurants.

Saturday

Simon bought cakes for the family next door. V. well behaved. We left.

[Beside photo:] In Conques we ate at the Ch[ar?][magne?] rest[aurant?] [Alyst?] salads; had [chestnut?] crème [brulee?] good coffee. It [rained?] on + off [all] day. We walk[ed] a[round] the town enjoyed looking at [illegible]

captions: C'est à un voyage privilégié dans l'étage des tribunes de l'abbatiale que le visiteur est exceptionnellement convié depuis peu. Il y découvrira des perspectives inoubliables et percevra, par les jeux d'éclairage, la beauté plastique des chapiteaux romans.
pages.text:
the old buildings + shops. We drove on to [?] race thru hills + along [?] + rain. We had trouble remembering [where] the campsite [is]. It was no longer advertising camping [when] we got there.

White monks organised a short service in the abbey with psalms at noon. The metal work grills around the sanctuary were made of prisoners' chains. St. foy was prayed to for prisoners. The abbey has continued this theme by having writing + photos about hostages, torture + prisoners Today, since 1995, in Eritraya. 10,000 people have disappeared. Enforced national service is in a brutal army

Sunday

Rain in the night. Damp but no rain in the morning. Apparently the rain is the first they've had since June 10th.

We drove to Figeac, looked at the Rosetta Stone, had good coffee in the covered market & shopped. At 12 we arrived at John Hurman's & met his freind, Margaret Tucker. We had lunch on his Terrace, but, again (a (5 years ago) we had to move in because of rain during the meal. It was a lovely afternoon. We ended with playing Skittles. In the evening we went along old roads around __Cajarc Campsite__ **** There is a pool, a lovely walnut orchard, but the loo block is v old.
Monday
__Simon has found that 2 of the 4 brackets holding up the water tanks have slackened__, so after about 130 miles we found land with suitable hollows & Simon has parked the van over them & is under the van working on the brackets. We went to a supermarket & on towards David's. At Chambert we again stopped to buy glue to mend a broken drawer handle. We arrived & were made to feel very welcome. David & Sally like the Taj Mahal table. We parked the van overlooking the stupendous view from the bottom of the garden, away from the trees because there may be a storm. We played whist & looked at the stars. Sally has read there should be lots of

shooting stars for 3 days. Simon saw 3, I saw 1.
__Tuesday__
We set off about 10.20. for a walk to Treignac - Sally, Helen, Simon & I. On the way I had to ask at a house for water. At 12.45. we got there. David arrived with Maia & Orla & Jean & bikes. We had crepes & beer & swam in the lake. Simon & Jean biked home, the rest of us drove. We got very worried when Simon didn't arrive on the Brompton (David's 5 years) it had a puncture. We had lunch & Sally read a book. Simon & David mended things. I carted horse manure thro the barn to David's trees in the other field. David cooked risotto for supper.
__Wednesday__
Some went shopping. Maia & I took

Orla [?up] to see the white horses from the Camargue. The farmer arrived + we had a long chat. I was nervous with the horses so near. Orla failed to sleep.
After lunch everyone went off to swim to [?storm] lake Viam , with Jem cycling. I stayed behind + read + did more [?manure] + washing. Simon, David, Helen, Eve went to __Thursday__ a guitar concert in a church at 8.30. It was desperately hot, with no through draught. The east end of the church was wooden, painted with pilasters + niches to look classical. The ceiling was wooden barrel. Lovely music by Alain Rizoul at Rilhac Treignac, but way too hot.
    We are on the Plateau des Mille Vaches, which means plateau of a thousand villages, not a thousand cows.
    Simon has moved the van up to the front door so that we don't get stuck.
__Thursday__
Rain + storms in the night. We have to go to

bed in the dark. At about 11 enormous hornets come out of their nest in the tree + fly to the kitchen lights.
    Simon + Eve go to Intermarché. Adam arrives. Simon, Eve, Helen, David, Sally go for a walk in the afternoon + pick blackberries + get caught, just, in a thunderstorm. A lovely walk.
    We all went out to supper at Masseret. It was delicious food, even the cheese, + a good waiter. Simon's sides fell off under the table, he passed out in just shoe uppers.
__Friday__ more rain.
    9:15. after David had signed some papers we left. We bought bread at Chamboret, (where Simon revved up forward into a hidden bollard) + left, with Helen, for the motorway. Apart from my driving for 1½ hrs, Simon drove the whole way until we reached the Hermitage Hotel, Montreuil, 8.45 pm. At which point Simon asked Helen if she had her passport. She said she didn't, then she said she did + produced a brown envelope. When Simon opened it there was only her old passport inside it. Simon telephoned David who said a very rude word. He has now texted Simon all Helen's passport details.

Saturday 15 Aug It's a nice hotel, good breakfast. Clean, good quality bed linen + towels. We have set off towards the Channel Tunnel. Simon was fed up yesterday - mob screen cracked, he drove forward into an unseen bollard at the bread shop. And no passport for Helen. But the rest of then trip seems to have gone smoothly. Jams on road out of Kent + M25. But we saw Charles + Jenny (new digs) at Oxford, on the way home.

__Friday 4th Sept__
Simon + [?Tinder] drove across to Cambridge, bad road works on A14. Arrived 1.30. in time for Annette's Memorial lunch. The day before Eve had been at the FCO for [?Wasbuton] Room dedication + then travelled from London to Cambridge by train with Beth, Ted, [?Aetex] + Edith Alston. John had treated us all before hand to lunch at Farmers' Club. David + [?Natalise] saw him to his bus stop for the train. On Thursday morning a taxi had taken us out to the newly laid stone on her ashes next to [?Pearl], + we talked about Annette in the church. The Lucy Cavendish

[...C]ollege Memorial went well, with hymns, Amazing Grace + I bow to thee by my country + Beth gave one of the speeches.
	The FCO concentrated on her trail-blazing as a woman, Sir Richard Dales spoke about [her?] as a diplomat, Dr. Jane [?Renfrews] about her work in LCC, Beth about Bosnian women + the family + Pauline Perry of LCC. Then tea.
	Then Simon + I set out back westwards to __the [?Arlingworth] Lodge Campsite__, next to the village.
__Saturday__
	We chatted in bed for ages + then set off on a bike ride a disused railway, all the way to Brixworth where we had fish + chips at the pub. The saxon church 640 AD is very impressive. Beautiful stone. Apparently in C5 saints remains in Rome were moved out of the catacombs to under city churches because of Lombarian invaders. Here at Brixworth, they re-created the same ideas as the Romans - under ground passageways

for pilgrims to view relics, reminiscent of catacombs. The church had an ambulatory crypt originally covered, now just looking liked a moat. Back along Brampton Valley Cyclepath. The blackberries are not good, but the path has a nice, distinctive design for its benches - lovely mosaics. We walked thro the tunnel & needed lights. 18m.
__Sunday 6th Sept__
 We went on another, 10m. bike ride, the other way, to Market Harborough. We met a pleasant Vietnamese & French couple on a bridge. The tunnel was worse. Good-looking swings on the outskirts of M.H. Lunch back at the van. Tinder pulled Simon off the bike.
  We set off for home just before 3 in case Naomi needed help after Walter's tongue-tie op.

April 2016
 We went for a walk along the canal in the Black Country with Pill, Nana, Peter, Eleni & Conrad. It was cold. We had tea in the van
__June 3rd Friday__
 Simon, Eve & Tinder left at about 3.30 Ombersley
 We reached [?B.rnary Ho] [?farmyard] campsite ** near Holt Fleet, Worcs. after about 40 min. £[?] per night for us all. lovely sunshine all evening. I took far too many cloths when we walked down to the R. Severn. A super walk. Old cart tracks. Views of banks of trees. We camped next to a fenced off pond/lake. Fishermen arrived at 7 the next morning. lots of them came & we saw one catch a big fish. It's a square field with loos & barn. 3 other vans/caravans.
 We left at 8.30. & bought a feast at Leggs including Bromyard Brie, which I just to my annoyance is actually made in Worcestershire.

Moor End Farm
Ashperton

On to the farm where we looked at the excavations for the big new hop barn structure
William complained about the engineer doing detailed design of the barn.
	Freya met us & we walked up to the yurt. I had given Jenny + Nina + Briony presents from Japan. Gilda + Sally + their twin boys were visiting for the w[ee]kend. The yurt is being opened. Freya is going to manage Charles' + Jenny's wedding – 'if they get to marry at Belmont Abbey + reception at the Farm. Belmont so uncertain....
	We moved off for lunch down a lane near Belmont Abbey. All local people very helpful. Lots of may out + elderflo[wers]

After lunch we looked around the Abbey. Charles put on a clean, very creased shirt & covered it up with a jacket. Charles + Jenny had an interview with Father Nick who says they may get married at Belmont Abbey. Hooray! Next June or July.
	After tea, when Sonja sounded out the hotel there, we left for Moor End Farm Campsite. The farmer lent us a map + we walked thru fields of sheep + broad beans to Ashperton Castle Moat + looked round the outside of the church. The nice chair of PCC, who came to this part of Herefordshire from the other side to be a milk maid a long time ago told us [the] Eng. Heritage will only let them drain + repair [1292] Moat if they don't use mechanical digger, so it is deteriorating.
	Lovely field. A Canal (built 1790–1849)
2 phase build

Gloucester–Hereford canal, lots of Navvies buried in the churchyard.
Ashperton tunnel unstable.
    We left early to get back to Sunday church at St. G.  Simon lost van key.

__Wednesday July 6th__
9.15. Simon, Finder & Eve drove to Charles' & Jenny's new house in Oxford, Frenchay Rd off Woodstock Rd.  A week ago he beat the nasty woman next door in planning permission for a window.  House full of workmen working on shell.  Rooms clear.  V. exciting.                                          Haypr
On to deliver lights Watlington & [?send] Hinley.
On to Peter & Nana's house at Finsbury Pk.
Our last visit before they move after July 19th.
We slept in van & had breakfast with Nana & Thomas.  Peter in Wales & SW with Conrad, Eleni & [?Satter]-in-law Chhatrapati.  Nana was doing a dance exhibition which we had to miss.  After watching a bit of Murray at Wimbledon we went to the Merchant Taylors, gorgeous, & had a BBQ in courtyard put on by [illegible].  We (Chad) had invited 3 designers.

We tried to speak to as many designers as possible.  I sat next to a very nice Irishman who did sea diving & had 3 daughters & a (small) salvage ~~card~~ company.  He did the joinery on the QE2.

__Thursday__
    It's been so hot today & yesterday we've had to keep the chocolate in the fridge.
We went to the end of year London Show.
Jason (the designer's husband) gave us tickets.  It was WOW!
A man on [?Bham] stand had a stool made of felt – actually plastic bottle felt.  Compacted.  Simon liked the texture & wants to make lights out of it.
Another man wants to cut down on carbon footprint of light-making.  He puts maple chips
                                                              ← mushroom
    maple                                                         mould
                                                                 spores.
in a mould &
Ingots in a mould                     + heat 200
like dry rot.                              for 20 min.

the mould grows & grows into the shape of a tho light (shade). or whatever. To kill the mould you heat it when you get it out of the mould. It's water & fire resistant. (A company in the US is using huge warehouses to make packaging materials of Ikea).
Another man had made an iphone speaker out of bamboo. Magnifying the sound without electricity.
Another student with shakey hands had a hot water boiling water dispenser so old people didn't have to lift a kettle.
    We drove on & found a carpark next to St. Mary's Headley & Box Hill, a well-kept church, for lunch.
    On to see Arathna & Godfrey. Eng 12. & Evans 10, spent all our visit playing with Tinder.

captions:
PRAYER OF ST RICHARD OF CHICHESTER
Thanks be to thee, my Lord Jesus Christ,
For all the benefits which Thou hast given me,
For all the pains and insults
Which Thou hast borne for me,
O most merciful Redeemer,
Friend and Brother,
May I know Thee more clearly,
Love Thee more dearly
And follow Thee more nearly.
CHICHESTER CATHEDRAL
pages.text:
We are at a farm above Steyning. We walked 1½ miles to the Chequers where we had good ham & chips but no pudding.
    Tinder has been very good.
Friday
    We visited Simon & Claudia Ogden for elevenses. They were very welcoming. Then we joined the Gravestocks who gave us a delicious lunch. We went for a walk through a park & watched water skiing & saw the sea.
    At △ we left for our campsite The Ship & Anchor at Littlehampton ***
There's a bike path to Arundel, but it's very overgrown.

captions: Arundel R.C. Cathedral. Gate of mercy. Nice church.
Philip Howard this dog.
pages.text:
Saturday.
We set off, cycling along the raised embankment next to the R. Arun. Hardgoing because of June grasses & thistles. We could see Arundel Castle in
[photo of Arundel Cathedral]
Arundel R.C. Cathedral. Gate of mercy.
Nice church.
Philip Howard
this dog.

captions: The Chagall window (detail)- see 13 Chichester Cathedral
pages.text:
the distance which I first mistook for another cement works. It got nearer & nearer. The wind was pushing us.
  We had a coffee & I bought lavender for Venetia whom we've visiting tomorrow in her new house. We also bought pastries for supper. etc
  We caught a train back to Ford Station along the road to the campsite pub for lund which we had booked for 12.30. Good [?Affo] steak & kidney pie.
  After a nap we drove to Chichester & looked at the shops & went to Evensong. Who Should turn up but the Roses! We stopped at Bognor Regis (George V bugger Bognor!) on the way back to the campsite for supper in the van.
  5 0p each tokens for showers. Good Showers.
[photo: The Chagall window (detail)- see 13 Chichester Cathedral]

Sunday

We visited Venetia & David in their new home "The New House" for coffee on our way home. Very smart bath rooms. Lovely home.
Soon after leaving the van mil o metre passed from 99,000 to 100,000. (Just under 4,000 per year.) On our way home to watch Boring Murray in Wimbledon Finals.
~~Friday~~ Monday August 12th

Thomas & family in France. Peter & family we've just spent a day with in new house in Tufnell Green. John has started at Bristol one week into ICU job. Charles living with future mo-in-law in Witney until Oxford ho- ready.
12.30 Simon & Eve, Tindse leave for farm. Jam on motorway, lunch, arrive farm at 3. Simon & Eve help bind hop stems around sticks & pull up weeds. Discuss small hop-drying frame with Andy - Eve suggest Queen ocluders. Watch bulldozers changing bank from J to V.

Joseph has won 5 rosettes at a gymkhana yesterday. A big break-time in his confidence. We play draughts. Or gorgeous weather Simon & I join adults at wood-work place for pyjya & chat. 2 firemen - Mr & Mrs.
Tuesday Aug 13th
We come to countryside for p.g. At 7.15 lorries arrive. By 7.45 there is one bull dozer or 2 ? 2 dumper trucks, 3 lorries delivering gravel outside (above Will + Kenny's caravan) & 3 grandchildren having break fast in our van. Simon helps William on farm. Freya takes children & me to Barnyard, shopping & Library. Back at farm physio arrives for Joseph in pool. Eve swims. lunch. Then we leave.
3.45. we reach Polwigo Campsite Llanwrthwl near Rhayader & Elan valley. We go on a lovely walk, small village.
Then read; sparkling wine, supper. Gorgeous sunshine. Cambrian Mtns.
